evidence: README mandates explicit contract governance and test obligations for protocol/StarTram contract surfaces, including deterministic unit tests and per-contract lifecycle-policy assertions.

The listed contract definition files are pure contract-surface declarations (`protocolContractCatalogSpecs`, `startramContractCatalogSpecs`) and expose no in-file testability hooks or invariant checks, so regressions in contract IDs/metadata can only be caught indirectly.
Contract entries combine raw literals for IDs and descriptors in the same file, creating a high-risk, high-impact surface for subtle drift without local coverage.

findings.summary: Critical protocol/StarTram contract descriptors are mutable by edit but have no enforced local invariants or deterministic tests in this declaration scope.
evidence: README requires deterministic unit test coverage for contract surfaces but the listed files are high-impact contract registries with no local invariant coverage.

`protocolContractCatalogSpecs` and `startramContractCatalogSpecs` currently return raw descriptor literals without asserting required shape/compatibility properties (ID format, action/namespace consistency, metadata presence, lifecycle policy expectations).
The same README section also calls out explicit per-contract lifecycle-policy assertions for changed catalog entries, but this batch’s files provide no direct testable check.
findings.suggestion: Add contract-surface tests in `goseg/protocol/contracts` that validate every generated catalog entry for required invariants (non-empty ID/namespace/action/message/name, valid metadata key mapping, and lifecycle policy assertions), and make those tests part of the required change set whenever these files are modified.

findings.summary: Contract governance currently depends on README/CI instructions instead of executable contract-compatibility assertions, creating fragility for cross-module compatibility confidence.
evidence: The batch’s governing doc explicitly says protocol contract changes must pass runtime contract gates and contract-specific test matrices, but the files themselves do not embed validation points that protect compatibility semantics at declaration time.

The contract API IDs/names/descriptions are encoded as literal constants and descriptors that are central to runtime command compatibility, yet there is no local compile-time/runtime self-check pattern in these files.
This creates a structural testing gap where a contract-surface change can appear to satisfy docs while bypassing behavioral cross-checks until later CI or downstream runtime usage.
findings.suggestion: Introduce an explicit contract metadata validation helper in `contracts` package (or expand existing catalog initialization) that enforces compatibility rules (e.g., required metadata completeness, deprecated/active consistency, and namespace/action uniqueness) and is exercised by focused tests, reducing reliance on external process documents.
